title: Batch & group effects
text: shifts numeric vector length n_batches moves every target feature batch SD-multiple. three batches indistinguishable fourth shifted hard – classic outlier plate:
code: batched <- add_batch_effect(syn, n_batches = 4,                             shifts = c(0, 0, 0, 3), seed = 42) table(batched$data$batch) #>  #> batch1 batch2 batch3 batch4  #>     75     75     75     75 ## syn data PCA  num_syn <- syn$data[, vapply(syn$data, is.numeric, logical(1))] pca_syn <- prcomp(num_syn, scale. = TRUE)$x  df_syn <- data.frame(pca_syn, Species = syn$data$Species)  p1 = ggplot(df_syn, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Species)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Original Simulated Data") +   theme_minimal()  ### batched data PCA  num_batched <- batched$data[, vapply(batched$data, is.numeric, logical(1))] pca_batched <- prcomp(num_batched, scale. = TRUE)$x  df_batched <- data.frame(pca_batched, batch = batched$data$batch, Species = batched$data$Species)  p2 = ggplot(df_batched, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Species, shape = batch)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Uniform batch effect (batch4 = +3 SD)",        x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +   theme_minimal() +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") # Clean, distinct colors for your 4 batches  p1 | p2

title: Batch & group effects
text: Real batch effects hit every feature equally. shifts = "spike_slab" draws per-feature shift batch – hit ~ Bernoulli(shift_prob) N(0, shift_sd) – leaving clean reference_batch. proportions gives unequal batch sizes. realistic ’omics signature scales wide matrices. recorded shift_matrix ground truth: hand batched data correction method check well recovers unshifted values.
code:
batched_ss <- add_batch_effect(   syn,    n_batches = 4,    shifts = "spike_slab",   shift_prob = 0.5, # per feature probability being shifted   shift_sd = 1, # the SD shift   proportions = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1), # relative size of each batch; sums to 1   reference_batch = 1,    seed = 6 )  cat("Batch sizes
") #> Batch sizes table(batched_ss$data$batch)                       # unequal sizes #>  #> batch1 batch2 batch3 batch4  #>    120     90     60     30 cat("
Sift Matrix: Batch by featuer in SD units of shift from reference

") #>  #> Sift Matrix: Batch by featuer in SD units of shift from reference round(batched_ss$batch_effect$shift_matrix, 2)     # ground-truth shifts (row 1 = clean) #>        Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width #> batch1         0.00        0.00         0.00        0.00 #> batch2         0.00        0.00         0.74       -0.11 #> batch3         0.00       -0.59         0.00        0.29 #> batch4         0.07       -0.81         0.00       -0.27

title: Batch & group effects
text: add_group_effect() adds named grouping factor whose levels move different features different directions – genuinely nominal factor single-latent copula produce (categorical phenotype injection can ordinal gradient). Two ways specify effect.

title: Batch & group effects
text: named list keyed level, named numeric per-feature shifts (SD units). Omitted level = reference; omitted feature = 0. clay small petals silt wide sepals – two different features, ordering:
code:
g <- add_group_effect(   syn, name = "soil", levels = c("clay", "sand", "silt"),   probs = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2), # relative size of each batch; sums to 1   effects = list(clay = c(Petal.Length = -0.9),                  silt = c(Sepal.Width  =  1.0)),   seed = 3 )  cat("Median Petal Length by Soil type
") #> Median Petal Length by Soil type tapply(g$data$Petal.Length, g$data$soil, median)   # clay is low #>    clay    sand    silt  #> 2.89773 4.50000 4.50000 cat("
Median Sepal Width by Soil type
") #>  #> Median Sepal Width by Soil type tapply(g$data$Sepal.Width,  g$data$soil, median)   # silt is high #>     clay     sand     silt  #> 3.000000 3.000000 3.420095

title: Batch & group effects
text: named-list matrix infeasible 11,000-feature panel. generative mode says “level perturbs proportion features effect-size distribution” – spike--slab drawn independently per level, level gets random signature: level draws random subset, perturbed feature sets barely overlap levels – defining property nominal factor. Affected feature marginals become mixtures across groups, intended: group genuinely changes distribution.
code: ## Generate a fake omics data set omics <- as.data.frame(matrix(rnorm(300 * 500), 300, 500,                               dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("p", 1:500))))  ## Define a new variable `tissue` and add group effects gg <- add_group_effect(   omics, name = "tissue",    levels = c("liver", "muscle", "fat"),   probs = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2), # relative size of each batch; sums to 1   effect_prop = 0.10, ## probability that a feature is effected   effect_sd = 0.5,    seed = 42 )  ## Number of features effected by each level cat("Number of features influenced by each feature:\n") #> Number of features influenced by each feature: rowSums( attr(gg, "group_effect")$shift_matrix != 0)   # ~50 of 500 features per level #>  liver muscle    fat  #>     56     50     49

what: What the synthetica R package is for
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: synthetica generates synthetic copies tabular datasets preserve marginal distribution every column joint correlation structure across columns – without retaining row-level information input. supports mixed-type data (numeric, binary, categorical) designed feel like small, self-contained utility reach need: shareable version cohort data, scrubbed individual rows Teaching/test datasets mimic statistical properties real data without exposing Power analyses known ground-truth effect baked Stress tests batch-correction normalisation pipelines package exports four functions: [simulate_dataset()][simulate_dataset] – main copula simulator, optional phenotype injection per-level fitting (stratify_by) [add_batch_effect()][add_batch_effect] – post-processor introduces synthetic technical batch structure [add_group_effect()][add_group_effect] – post-processor adds designed nominal grouping factor [simulate_longitudinal()][simulate_longitudinal] – wrapper repeated-measures / panel data article getting-started tour: baseline simulation, recovery checks, stratification, integer/discrete fidelity, real proteomics example. articles cover rest: Injecting phenotypes – continuous, binary, categorical traits correlated chosen features Batch & group effects – technical batches designed biological groups Longitudinal data – repeated measures via simulate_longitudinal()

what: Simulate a synthetic copy of the iris dataset
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: Generate synthetic copy 300 rows, doubling size input. - note can ask n
code: sim <- simulate_dataset(iris,                          n = nrow(iris) * 2,                          seed = 42,                          verbose = FALSE)

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > Simulate a synthetic copy of the iris dataset
what: sim is a list of class synthetica_sim.
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: synthetic data.frame lives sim$data: 300 rows 5 columns, shape input row-level information retained.
code: class(sim) #> [1] "synthetica_sim"

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > Simulate a synthetic copy of the iris dataset
what: There are various attributes in the sim object:
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
code: names(sim) #>  [1] "data"                  "marginals"             "corr"                  #>  [4] "missingness"           "inject_truth"          "dropped_cols"          #>  [7] "dropped_near_constant" "dropped_high_missing"  "audit"                 #> [10] "call"

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > Simulate a synthetic copy of the iris dataset
what: The data attribute is the synthetic dataset itself:
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
code: dim(sim$data) #> [1] 300   5

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > Simulate a synthetic copy of the iris dataset
what: The audit attribute summarises what was dropped or collapsed by the simulations guardrails:
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
code: sim$audit #> [1] "privacy: dropped 0 near-constant column(s); dropped 0 column(s) with missingness > 90%; collapsed rare levels in 0 categorical column(s)"

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > How well does the simulated match the input?
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: Marginal recovery: input vs simulated (numeric columns)
code: input_num <- iris[, vapply(iris,    is.numeric, logical(1))] sim_num   <- sim$data[, vapply(sim$data, is.numeric, logical(1))]  summary_compare <- data.frame(   variable = names(input_num),   in_median = vapply(input_num, median, numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE),   sim_median= vapply(sim_num,   median, numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE),   in_sd     = vapply(input_num, sd,     numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE),   sim_sd    = vapply(sim_num,   sd,     numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE),   in_miss   = vapply(input_num, function(x) mean(is.na(x)), numeric(1)),   sim_miss  = vapply(sim_num,   function(x) mean(is.na(x)), numeric(1)) )  summary_compare |>   kable(digits = 3,         caption = "Marginal recovery: input vs simulated (numeric columns)") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > How well does the simulated match the input?
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: Species level frequencies
code: # Categorical recovery (Species) data.frame(   level      = levels(iris$Species),   input_prop = as.numeric(prop.table(table(iris$Species))),   sim_prop   = as.numeric(prop.table(table(sim$data$Species))) ) |>   kable(digits = 3, caption = "Species level frequencies") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > How well does the simulated match the input?
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: Gaussian copula preserves rank (Spearman) correlations construction:
code: in_cor  <- cor(input_num, use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman") sim_cor <- cor(sim_num,   use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "spearman")  # Frobenius distance, normalised fro <- norm(in_cor - sim_cor, type = "F") / norm(in_cor, type = "F") cat(sprintf("Spearman correlation Frobenius distance (relative): %.4f\n", fro)) #> Spearman correlation Frobenius distance (relative): 0.0990

previous_headings: Simple Simulation: > How well does the simulated match the input?
what: Pairwise comparison for the strong pairs
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: Pairwise Spearman correlations: input vs simulated strong botanical correlations among iris features reproduced closely.
code: data.frame(   pair       = c("Sepal.L vs Sepal.W", "Sepal.L vs Petal.L",                  "Sepal.L vs Petal.W", "Sepal.W vs Petal.L",                  "Sepal.W vs Petal.W", "Petal.L vs Petal.W"),   input_cor  = c(in_cor[1,2],  in_cor[1,3],  in_cor[1,4],                  in_cor[2,3],  in_cor[2,4],  in_cor[3,4]),   sim_cor    = c(sim_cor[1,2], sim_cor[1,3], sim_cor[1,4],                  sim_cor[2,3], sim_cor[2,4], sim_cor[3,4]) ) |>   kable(digits = 3, caption = "Pairwise Spearman correlations: input vs simulated") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: Iris textbook case single categorical column – Species – drives variability continuous columns. single Gaussian copula fitted across 150 rows captures global marginal variable linear correlation across , three within-species clusters blur together: synthetic data won’t quite reproduce characteristic three-cluster shape PCA. fix stratify simulation: pass stratify_by = "Species" simulator fits separate copula within level, concatenates per-stratum sims. Compare PCA:  single-copula version produces three blurry overlapping clusters; stratified version reproduces crisp three-cluster shape real iris data. Per-stratum medians proportions preserved within sampling noise: Per-species median recovery (stratified simulation) reach stratify_by: categorical column strong effect continuous columns care preserving within-group joint structure (just global marginal global correlation). stratum needs least min_strata_n rows (default 30); smaller strata skipped warning.
code: sim_strat <- simulate_dataset(iris, n = 300, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE,                               stratify_by = "Species")  # Per-level row counts in the output table(sim_strat$data$Species) #>  #>     setosa versicolor  virginica  #>        100        100        100  # Per-level marginals and correlation matrices are kept separately names(sim_strat$marginals) #> [1] "setosa"     "versicolor" "virginica" dim(sim_strat$corr$setosa) #> [1] 4 4 par(mfrow = c(1, 3), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))  plot_iris_pca <- function(df, title) {   num <- df[, vapply(df, is.numeric, logical(1))]   ok  <- stats::complete.cases(num)   pca <- prcomp(num[ok, ], scale. = TRUE)   cols <- c(setosa = "#E41A1C", versicolor = "#377EB8", virginica = "#4DAF4A")   plot(pca$x[, 1:2], col = cols[as.character(df$Species[ok])],        pch = 19, cex = 0.7,        xlab = sprintf("PC1 (%.1f%%)", 100 * pca$sdev[1]^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2)),        ylab = sprintf("PC2 (%.1f%%)", 100 * pca$sdev[2]^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2)),        main = title)   legend("topright", legend = names(cols), col = cols, pch = 19, bty = "n", cex = 0.8) }  plot_iris_pca(iris,           "Real iris (n = 150)") plot_iris_pca(sim$data,       "Single copula (n = 300)") plot_iris_pca(sim_strat$data, "Stratified by Species (n = 300)") do.call(rbind, lapply(levels(iris$Species), function(sp) {   data.frame(     Species             = sp,     in_Petal_L_median   = median(iris$Petal.Length[iris$Species == sp], na.rm = TRUE),     sim_Petal_L_median  = median(sim_strat$data$Petal.Length[sim_strat$data$Species == sp], na.rm = TRUE),     in_Petal_W_median   = median(iris$Petal.Width[iris$Species == sp], na.rm = TRUE),     sim_Petal_W_median  = median(sim_strat$data$Petal.Width[sim_strat$data$Species == sp], na.rm = TRUE)   ) })) |>   kable(digits = 3,         caption = "Per-species median recovery (stratified simulation)") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: synthetica detects column types preserves round-trip. Whole-number columns come back integers (interpolated floats), correlations among binary / ordered-discrete columns recovered rather attenuated. mtcars compact mixed-type example: Storage class: input vs simulated (count-like columns stay integer) Count-like columns (cyl, hp, gear, carb, vs, ) returned integer; genuinely continuous measurements (mpg, disp, …) stay double. hood, low-cardinality integers keep exact support binary / ordered columns latent correlations recovered via tetrachoric / biserial (binary) polychoric / polyserial (ordinal) corrections – 0/1 ordered-factor column’s associations survive copula round-trip.
code: sim_cars <- simulate_dataset(mtcars, n = nrow(mtcars), seed = 42,                              verbose = FALSE)  data.frame(   column     = names(mtcars),   input_type = vapply(mtcars,        function(x) class(x)[1], character(1)),   sim_type   = vapply(sim_cars$data, function(x) class(x)[1], character(1)) ) |>   kable(caption = "Storage class: input vs simulated (count-like columns stay integer)") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

what: A real ’omics example: Olink NPX data
title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: chunks illustrate workflow real proteomics dataset – npx_data1 OlinkAnalyze, fetched top vignette. dataset long format – one row per (sample, assay). Convert wide row sample column assay: First 4 samples x 5 assays wide NPX matrix Simulate synthetic copy: Quick marginal-recovery check random subset assays: Now apply four-batch effect top synthetic Olink data:  headline use case package ’omics data: realistic correlation structure, realistic missingness, plus controllable, known batch effect stress-testing downstream methods. See Batch & group effects article full add_batch_effect() / add_group_effect() API (spike--slab shifts, unequal batch sizes, designed biological groups).
code:
str(npx_data1, max.level = 1) #> tibble [29,440 × 17] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) length(unique(npx_data1$SampleID)) #> [1] 158 length(unique(npx_data1$Assay)) #> [1] 184 # npx_data1 is a tibble; coerce to a base data.frame so reshape(), rownames<-, # and positional subsetting behave predictably. long  <- as.data.frame(npx_data1)[, c("SampleID", "Assay", "NPX")] wide  <- stats::reshape(long, idvar = "SampleID", timevar = "Assay",                         direction = "wide") # tidy up the column names ("NPX.<assay>" -> "<assay>") names(wide)[-1] <- sub("^NPX\.", "", names(wide)[-1]) rownames(wide)  <- wide$SampleID wide$SampleID   <- NULL dim(wide) #> [1] 158 184 wide[1:4, 1:5] |>   kable(digits = 3, caption = "First 4 samples x 5 assays of the wide NPX matrix") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11) sim_olink <- simulate_dataset(wide, n = nrow(wide), seed = 42, verbose = FALSE) cat("Input dims :", nrow(wide), "x", ncol(wide), "
",     "Sim dims   :", nrow(sim_olink$data), "x", ncol(sim_olink$data), "
",     "Dropped by guardrails:", length(sim_olink$dropped_cols),     "(", length(sim_olink$dropped_high_missing), "high-missing,",     length(sim_olink$dropped_near_constant), "near-constant)
") #> Input dims : 158 x 184  #>  Sim dims   : 158 x 184  #>  Dropped by guardrails: 0 ( 0 high-missing, 0 near-constant) common  <- intersect(names(wide), names(sim_olink$data)) in_med  <- vapply(wide[, common, drop = FALSE], median, numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE) sim_med <- vapply(sim_olink$data, median, numeric(1), na.rm = TRUE) cat(sprintf("median  recovery (Pearson cor across %d assays): %.4f
",             length(common), cor(in_med, sim_med))) #> median  recovery (Pearson cor across 184 assays): 0.9944 sim_olink <- add_batch_effect(sim_olink, n_batches = 4,                               shifts = c(0, 0.05, -0.05, 0.6),                               seed = 1)  num    <- sim_olink$data[, vapply(sim_olink$data, is.numeric, logical(1))] ok     <- stats::complete.cases(num) pca    <- prcomp(num[ok, ], scale. = TRUE) cols   <- c("steelblue", "darkorange", "forestgreen", "firebrick") plot(pca$x[, 1:2],      col  = cols[sim_olink$data$batch[ok]],      pch  = 19, cex = 0.8,      xlab = sprintf("PC1 (%.1f%%)", 100 * pca$sdev[1]^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2)),      ylab = sprintf("PC2 (%.1f%%)", 100 * pca$sdev[2]^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2)),      main = "Olink synthetic copy + simulated batch effect") legend("topright", legend = levels(sim_olink$data$batch),        col = cols, pch = 19, bty = "n")

title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: simulate_dataset() applies small set guardrails fitting, synthetic output real participant data memorise individuals. defaults tuned cohort-sized data: passed named list privacy argument; marginal_resolution top-level argument. example, loosen dependence structure correlation noise: sim$audit prints one-line summary dropped collapsed, can capture manifest sharing synthetic output.
code: sim_dp <- simulate_dataset(   iris, n = 150, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE,   marginal_resolution = 128,   privacy = list(min_cell_size = 10, noise_on_corr = 0.10) )

title: synthetica: a walkthrough
text: Binary phenotypes retain single, predictable thresholding factor phi(c) / sqrt(p(1-p)) round-trip; generating latent spliced copula designed correlation otherwise captured exactly. See inject_truth$<name>$expected_sim_cor. Quantitative phenotypes targeting many highly-correlated features attenuate mildly due rank-deficient inversion target correlation matrix. Unordered categorical features > 2 levels introduce ordering- dependent latent correlations. Marginal level frequencies preserved exactly; cross-correlations approximate. (Ordered discrete columns polychoric-corrected.) causal / DAG structure – joint correlations preserved. High-dimensional longitudinal ’omics (features x timepoints thousands) scope simulate_longitudinal(); see Longitudinal data article tractable regime.

title: Longitudinal data (repeated measures)
text: plain copula treats every row independent, wrong repeated measures: subject’s value one timepoint correlated value next. simulate_longitudinal() handles reshaping wide format (one row per subject, column per feature x time), reusing simulate_dataset(), reshaping back. just name id time columns. turns within-subject temporal autocorrelation ordinary -column correlation, everything applies free: per-timepoint marginals capture trajectory shape (e.g. growth), missingness model reproduces dropout, time-invariant covariates ride along single columns.

title: Longitudinal data (repeated measures)
text: datasets::ChickWeight 50 chicks weighed 12 timepoints, time-invariant Diet:
code: data(ChickWeight) sim <- simulate_longitudinal(ChickWeight, id = "Chick", time = "Time",                              seed = 1, verbose = FALSE)  class(sim)        # "synthetica_long" #> [1] "synthetica_long" sim$static        # "Diet" auto-detected (constant within Chick) #> [1] "Diet" sim$features      # "weight" (time-varying) #> [1] "weight" head(sim$data) #>   weight Time Chick Diet #> 1     43    0 subj1    1 #> 2     51    2 subj1    1 #> 3     61    4 subj1    1 #> 4     72    6 subj1    1 #> 5     84    8 subj1    1 #> 6     87   10 subj1    1

title: Longitudinal data (repeated measures)
text: timepoint marginal, mean trajectory emerges automatically: Mean weight timepoint: real vs synthetic
code: data.frame(   Time = sort(unique(ChickWeight$Time)),   real = round(tapply(ChickWeight$weight,    ChickWeight$Time, mean), 1),   syn  = round(tapply(sim$data$weight, sim$data$Time, mean), 1) ) |>   kable(caption = "Mean weight by timepoint: real vs synthetic") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

what: Trajectories look like growth, not noise
title: Longitudinal data (repeated measures)
code: op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1)) cols <- c("#E41A1C", "#377EB8", "#4DAF4A", "#984EA3") spaghetti <- function(df, title) {   plot(NA, xlim = range(df$Time), ylim = range(df$weight, na.rm = TRUE),        xlab = "Time", ylab = "weight", main = title)   for (ch in unique(df$Chick)) {     d <- df[df$Chick == ch, ]     lines(d$Time, d$weight, col = adjustcolor(cols[as.integer(d$Diet)[1]], 0.5))   } } spaghetti(ChickWeight, "Real") spaghetti(sim$data,    "Synthetic") par(op)

title: Longitudinal data (repeated measures)
text: point wide-format trick subject’s weights across time stay coupled. Compare day-0 vs day-20 correlation, aligned chick: Chicks died early reproduce realistic dropout – (subject, time) rows observed feature absent synthetic long output, just input.
code: pair_cor <- function(df, t0, t1) {   a <- df[df$Time == t0, c("Chick", "weight")]   b <- df[df$Time == t1, c("Chick", "weight")]   m <- merge(a, b, by = "Chick")   cor(m[[2]], m[[3]], use = "complete.obs") } c(real = round(pair_cor(ChickWeight, 0, 20), 3),   syn  = round(pair_cor(sim$data,    0, 20), 3)) #>   real    syn  #> -0.320 -0.354

title: Longitudinal data (repeated measures)
text: recipe covers: /RCTs – two timepoints become baseline / followup columns; pre-post correlation treatment shift preserved. Crossover trials – one column per period. Modest biomarker panels – handful analytes several visits.

title: Longitudinal data (repeated measures)
text: wide frame features x timepoints columns, approach tractable regime (hundred columns); warning fires beyond . High-dimensional longitudinal ’omics (thousands features x several timepoints) needs different method – subject-level random effects autoregressive temporal residual structure – scope . time grid set unique observed time values, genuinely irregular continuous times binned common grid calling.

title: Injecting phenotypes
text: Beyond reproducing input, simulate_dataset() can append new synthetic columns – phenotypes – correlated chosen subset features user-defined effect size. useful generating teaching datasets, power analyses, method benchmarks known ground-truth signal. entry inject named list describing trait. effect controls strongly correlates targets, either standardized mean_shift target r_squared latent scale. Three phenotype types supported: "quantitative", "binary", "categorical". iris + three injected phenotypes
code: sim <- simulate_dataset(   iris, n = 300, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE,   inject = list(     # 1. continuous trait, modest effect on every feature     rainfall = list(       type   = "quantitative", mean = 40, sd = 8,       effect = list(kind = "r_squared", min_r2 = 0.35, on = "all")     ),     # 2. binary trait (case/control), stronger effect on the petals     treated = list(       type   = "binary", prob = 0.40,       effect = list(kind = "r_squared", min_r2 = 0.3,                     on = c("Petal.Length", "Petal.Width"))     ),     # 3. ordinal categorical trait: clay -> sand -> silt gradient     soil = list(       type   = "categorical",       levels = c("clay", "sand", "silt"), probs = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2),       effect = list(kind = "r_squared", min_r2 = 0.3,                     on = c("Petal.Length", "Petal.Width"))     )   ) ) #> Warning in .attach_phenotypes(data, types, inject, verbose): phenotype #> 'rainfall': dropped 1 categorical target(s) -- not supported as effect targets #> in v1 #> Warning in .generate_phenotype(s, X_target, target_corr): phenotype effect #> infeasible: requested |corr| mean 0.592, clipped to 0.590  head(sim$data, 6) |>   kable(digits = 2, caption = "iris + three injected phenotypes") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

title: Injecting phenotypes
code: fit = lm(Petal.Length ~ rainfall + soil + treated, data = sim$data) a = anova(fit) eta_squared = a[, 2] / sum(a[,2]) names(eta_squared) = rownames(a) barplot(eta_squared[-4], col = c("blue4","green4","brown"),          main = "Variance Explained by Simulated Phenotype",          ylab = "variance explained")

title: Injecting phenotypes
code: p1 = sim$data |> ggplot(aes(x = rainfall, y = Petal.Length)) +   geom_point( aes(color = Species) ) +   # geom_smooth(method = "lm", aes(color = Species)) +   geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = 'y ~ x',                color = "grey", se = FALSE,                linetype = "dashed") +   theme_bw()  p2 = sim$data |> ggplot(aes(x = soil, y = Petal.Length, fill = soil)) +   geom_boxplot() +   scale_fill_brewer(palette  = "Set1") +   theme_bw()  p3 = sim$data |>    mutate(treated = as.factor(treated)) |>   ggplot(aes(x = treated, y = Petal.Length, fill = treated)) +   geom_boxplot() +   scale_fill_brewer(palette  = "Dark2") +   theme_bw()  p1 / (p2 | p3) + plot_layout(heights = c(2,1))

title: Injecting phenotypes
text: Every injected trait reproducible bookkeeping sim$inject_truth: Injected phenotype: realised vs expected effect
code: data.frame(   phenotype        = names(sim$inject_truth),   realised_cor     = vapply(sim$inject_truth,                             function(it) it$mean_abs_real_cor, numeric(1)),   expected_sim_cor = vapply(sim$inject_truth,                             function(it) it$expected_sim_cor, numeric(1)),   thresholding     = vapply(sim$inject_truth,                             function(it) it$binary_sim_factor, numeric(1)) ) |>   kable(digits = 3, caption = "Injected phenotype: realised vs expected effect") |>   kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, font_size = 11)

title: Injecting phenotypes
text: binary trait continuous latent thresholded 0/1. synthetica splices generating latent copula, designed correlation captured exactly – single, predictable thresholding factor phi(c) / sqrt(p(1-p)) remains. expected_sim_cor (latent target times factor) closely predicts realised point-biserial output.
code: c(prevalence = mean(sim$data$treated == 1),   is_integer = is.integer(sim$data$treated)) #> prevalence is_integer  #>       0.43       1.00

title: Injecting phenotypes
text: "categorical" phenotype ordinal trait: one latent correlated targets cut cumulative-probability thresholds probs ordered levels (first level low end). relationship ordinal, feature medians climb monotonically along levels: nominal factor – level perturbs different features different directions, ordering – use add_group_effect() (see Batch & group effects article). applied post-hoc, nominal factor survive single-latent copula round-trip.
code: prop.table(table(sim$data$soil))                       # ~ .5 / .3 / .2 #>  #>      clay      sand      silt  #> 0.4933333 0.2933333 0.2133333 tapply(sim$data$Petal.Length, sim$data$soil, median)   # clay < sand < silt #>     clay     sand     silt  #> 1.702760 4.879138 5.324760

title: Injecting phenotypes
text: Effects checked feasibility (rho' R_X^-1 rho <= 1). infeasible request – e.g. large effect many mutually-correlated targets – scaled largest achievable magnitude, warning naming requested vs achieved.

title: Authors and Citation
text: David . Hughes. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Hughes D (2026). synthetica: Simulate Synthetic Datasets Preserved Marginals Correlation Structure. R package version 0.1.0.
code: @Manual{,   title = {synthetica: Simulate Synthetic Datasets with Preserved Marginals and Correlation Structure},   author = {David A. Hughes},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0}, }

title: synthetica
text: synthetica generates privacy-preserving synthetic copies mixed-type tabular datasets via Gaussian copula. reproduces variable’s marginal distribution (means, SDs, level frequencies, missingness) joint correlation structure data – without ever retaining real participant row. marginal quantitative column stored quantile grid, never raw vector, synthetic output safe share even input real participant data. built share realistic--synthetic copies cohort ’omics datasets (Metabolon, Olink, Nightingale, SomaLogic), general-purpose works data.frame numeric matrix.

title: synthetica
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("hughesevoanth/synthetica")

title: synthetica
text: mixed-type data.frame works – numeric, binary, factor, without NAs. synthesise classic iris dataset. three species form well-separated clusters, fit separate copula per species stratify_by within-species correlation structure preserved. synthetic copy mirrors input’s column types (including Species factor) per-group marginals, leaking real rows.
code: library(synthetica)  syn <- simulate_dataset(iris, n = nrow(iris), seed = 42,                         stratify_by = "Species", verbose = FALSE)  head(syn$data) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width Species #> 1     4.900000    3.000000     1.400000         0.2  setosa #> 2     5.000000    3.300000     1.600000         0.2  setosa #> 3     5.086181    3.284624     1.500000         0.2  setosa #> 4     5.400000    3.800000     1.500000         0.2  setosa #> 5     5.366146    3.102106     1.400000         0.2  setosa #> 6     5.000000    3.243516     1.243057         0.1  setosa

title: synthetica
text: Projecting synthetic measurements real principal-component space shows synthetic data occupies manifold – three species clusters reproduced, just overall spread.
code: library(ggplot2)  num <- c("Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length", "Petal.Width") pca <- prcomp(iris[num], scale. = TRUE)  scores <- rbind(   data.frame(predict(pca, iris[num]),     Species = iris$Species,     source = "real"),   data.frame(predict(pca, syn$data[num]), Species = syn$data$Species, source = "synthetic") )  ggplot(scores, aes(PC1, PC2, colour = Species)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.8, size = 2) +   facet_wrap(~ source) +   labs(title = "iris: real vs. synthetic (projected into the real PC space)") +   theme_bw()

title: synthetica
text: vignette("intro", package = "synthetica") – full guided tour (iris + realistic Olink NPX proteomics example). ?simulate_dataset – privacy posture, phenotype injection, stratify_by documented detail.

title: synthetica
text: defaults chosen output safe share even input real participant data: minimum cell size 10, dropping columns 90% missingness, near-constant column removal, 128-knot quantile grid every quantitative marginal. See ?simulate_dataset full set privacy knobs.

title: synthetica
text: MIT (c) David . Hughes. See LICENSE.

what: Add a Synthetic Batch Effect to a Dataset — add_batch_effect
title: Add a Synthetic Batch Effect to a Dataset — add_batch_effect
text: Adds batch labels tabular dataset (optionally) applies per-batch shifts – SD units target feature – simulate technical batch effects common metabolomics, proteomics, 'omics pipelines.

title: Add a Synthetic Batch Effect to a Dataset — add_batch_effect
code: add_batch_effect(   x,   n_batches = 4L,   shifts = NULL,   shift_prob = 0.2,   shift_sd = 0.5,   reference_batch = 1L,   on = "all",   proportions = NULL,   batch_col = "batch",   level_names = NULL,   seed = NULL )

title: Add a Synthetic Batch Effect to a Dataset — add_batch_effect
text: x `data.frame` `synthetica_sim` object (return value [simulate_dataset()]). `synthetica_sim` passed, modification applied `$data` slot. n_batches Integer, number batches. Default 4. shifts Controls per-batch mean shift, **SD units** target feature. One : * `NULL` (default) – zeros; batch label added feature values   unchanged. * numeric vector length `n_batches` – *uniform* model: every   target feature batch `b` shifted `shifts[b] * sd(feature)`. * string `"spike_slab"` – *generative* model:   non-reference batch target feature, shift drawn   `rbinom(1, 1, shift_prob) * rnorm(1, 0, shift_sd)`. See `shift_prob`   `shift_sd`. shift_prob Numeric `[0, 1]`, per-feature probability "hit" shift `"spike_slab"` model (slab weight). Default 0.2. Ignored uniform model. shift_sd Positive numeric, SD slab `N(0, shift_sd)` `"spike_slab"` model, feature's SD units. Default 0.5. Ignored uniform model. reference_batch Integer index batch leave unshifted clean anchor `"spike_slab"` model (reference correct *toward*). Default `1L`. `NULL` perturbs batches. Ignored uniform model. One `""` (every numeric column), numeric fraction `(0, 1]` (random subset numeric columns), character vector feature names. Categorical non-numeric columns ignored. proportions Optional numeric vector length `n_batches` summing 1, giving relative size batch. `NULL` (default) means equally sized batches (within one row). batch_col Character, name new column. Must collide existing column names. Default `"batch"`. level_names Optional character vector length `n_batches`. `NULL` (default), levels `"batch1"`, `"batch2"`, ... seed Optional integer reproducible batch assignment, target selection (`` fraction), spike--slab shift draws.

title: Add a Synthetic Batch Effect to a Dataset — add_batch_effect
text: type `x`. `data.frame`: input batch   column appended target features shifted, carrying   `attr(., "batch_effect")` record. `synthetica_sim`: object   `$data` modified `$batch_effect` slot recording assignment   vector, realized shift matrix (batches x targets), targets,   `proportions`, model parameters.

title: Add a Synthetic Batch Effect to a Dataset — add_batch_effect
text: Two shift models supported. **uniform** model (pass `shifts` numeric vector length `n_batches`) moves every target feature batch SD-multiple. **spike--slab** model (pass `shifts = "spike_slab"`) draws per-feature shift batch features unaffected minority strongly perturbed – realistic, heterogeneous signature real batch effect. small post-processing helper operates either plain `data.frame` output [simulate_dataset()]. intended generating teaching/test data known batch structure stress-testing batch-correction methods. row randomly assigned batch. `proportions = NULL` batches equally sized (trailing rows distributed one-per-batch `n` divisible `n_batches`); otherwise sizes follow `proportions` via largest-remainder rounding still sum `n`. target feature `f` batch `b`, values shifted `shift[b, f] * sd(f, na.rm = TRUE)`, `shift[b, f]` comes chosen model. NAs left NA. Non-numeric columns passed unchanged.

title: Add a Synthetic Batch Effect to a Dataset — add_batch_effect
code: set.seed(1) df <- data.frame(   x = rnorm(400, 50, 10),   y = rlnorm(400, log(20), 0.5),   z = rnorm(400, 0, 1) )  # Uniform model: three indistinguishable batches and one with a +0.5 SD # shift on every numeric feature. df2 <- add_batch_effect(   df,   n_batches = 4,   shifts    = c(0.00, 0.05, -0.05, 0.50),   seed      = 42 ) table(df2$batch) #>  #> batch1 batch2 batch3 batch4  #>    100    100    100    100   # Spike-and-slab model: batch 1 is a clean reference; each other batch # perturbs ~20% of features. Unequal batch sizes via `proportions`. df3 <- add_batch_effect(   df,   n_batches   = 4,   shifts      = "spike_slab",   shift_prob  = 0.2,   shift_sd    = 0.5,   proportions = c(0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1),   seed        = 42 ) attr(df3, "batch_effect")$shift_matrix #>        x y z #> batch1 0 0 0 #> batch2 0 0 0 #> batch3 0 0 0 #> batch4 0 0 0

what: Add a Designed Nominal Group Effect to a Dataset — add_group_effect
title: Add a Designed Nominal Group Effect to a Dataset — add_group_effect
text: Adds categorical grouping factor (named levels chosen proportions) tabular dataset shifts numeric features **per-level x per-feature** amount, SD units. Unlike Gaussian-copula phenotype (see [simulate_dataset()]'s `inject`, models *ordinal* categorical along single latent gradient), genuinely **nominal** effect applied post-hoc: level moves subset features directions, factor reduced single ordering.

title: Add a Designed Nominal Group Effect to a Dataset — add_group_effect
code: add_group_effect(   x,   name,   levels,   probs = NULL,   effects = "spike_slab",   effect_prop = 0.1,   effect_sd = 0.5,   on = "all",   reference_level = NULL,   group_col = name,   seed = NULL )

title: Add a Designed Nominal Group Effect to a Dataset — add_group_effect
text: x `data.frame` `synthetica_sim` (return value [simulate_dataset()]). `synthetica_sim`, `$data` modified. name Character, label grouping factor (used default `group_col` recorded bookkeeping). levels Character vector (length >= 2, unique) level names. probs Optional numeric vector length `levels` summing 1 giving level proportions. `NULL` (default) means equal-sized groups. effects Either string `"spike_slab"` (generative, default) named list keyed level, named numeric vector per-feature shifts SD units (explicit). effect_prop Generative : per-feature probability level perturbs . Default 0.1. effect_sd Generative : SD `N(0, effect_sd)` slab (effect-size distribution), feature's SD units. Default 0.5. Generative : features eligible – `""` numeric columns, numeric fraction `(0, 1]` (random subset), character vector feature names. Default `""`. reference_level Optional level name left unshifted (clean "reference" group). `NULL` (default) perturbs every level. group_col Character, name new column. Must collide existing columns. Defaults `name`. seed Optional integer reproducible level assignment (generative mode) spike-slab draws.

title: Add a Designed Nominal Group Effect to a Dataset — add_group_effect
text: type `x`. `data.frame`: input grouping   column appended target features shifted, carrying   `attr(., "group_effect")` record. `synthetica_sim`: object   `$data` modified `$group_effect` slot recording realized   shift matrix (levels x targets), assignment, `probs`, model   parameters.

title: Add a Designed Nominal Group Effect to a Dataset — add_group_effect
text: Two ways specify effect: * **Generative** (`effects = "spike_slab"`, default) –   non-reference level, eligible feature hit probability   `effect_prop` , hit, shifted `N(0, effect_sd)` SD units. Designed   high-dimensional 'omics matrices (Olink, SomaScan, Metabolon)   explicit per-feature matrix infeasible. * **Explicit** – `effects` named list keyed level, element   named numeric vector per-feature shifts (SD units). Omitted level =   reference (shift); omitted feature = 0. Designed low-dimensional,   hand-tuned effects. shift changes group means, affected feature's marginal becomes mixture across groups – intended (group genuinely changes distribution) effect applied post-hoc rather folded copula.

title: Add a Designed Nominal Group Effect to a Dataset — add_group_effect
code: set.seed(1) df <- as.data.frame(matrix(rnorm(300 * 50), 300, 50,                            dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("p", 1:50))))  # Generative: a 3-level "tissue" factor; each level perturbs ~15% of the # 50 features, effects ~ N(0, 0.5 SD). Scales directly to 'omics widths. g <- add_group_effect(df, name = "tissue",                       levels = c("liver", "muscle", "fat"),                       probs  = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2),                       effect_prop = 0.15, effect_sd = 0.5, seed = 42) table(g$tissue) #>  #>  liver muscle    fat  #>    150     90     60  rowSums(attr(g, "group_effect")$shift_matrix != 0)  # features hit per level #>  liver muscle    fat  #>      3      9      4   # Explicit: hand-tuned effects on a couple of named features. g2 <- add_group_effect(df, name = "soil",                        levels = c("clay", "sand", "silt"),                        effects = list(clay = c(p1 = -0.8, p2 = -0.6),                                       silt = c(p1 =  0.9)),                        seed = 1)

what: Simulate a synthetic dataset preserving marginals and joint correlations — simulate_dataset
title: Simulate a synthetic dataset preserving marginals and joint correlations — simulate_dataset
text: Generate synthetic copy mixed-type tabular dataset via Gaussian copula. per-column marginals (means, SDs, level frequencies, missingness rate) joint correlation structure input preserved without retaining row-level information. Optionally inject synthetic phenotype columns user-specified marginal effect size chosen subset features.

title: Simulate a synthetic dataset preserving marginals and joint correlations — simulate_dataset
code: simulate_dataset(   data,   n = nrow(data),   col_types = NULL,   marginal_resolution = 128L,   missingness = c("preserve_pattern", "MCAR", "none"),   inject = NULL,   copula_regularization = "nearPD",   privacy = list(min_cell_size = 10L, noise_on_corr = 0, drop_near_constant = TRUE,     max_missingness = 0.9, force = FALSE),   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   stratify_by = NULL,   min_strata_n = 30L,   max_ordinal_levels = 10L )

title: Simulate a synthetic dataset preserving marginals and joint correlations — simulate_dataset
text: data `data.frame` numeric `matrix`. Samples rows, features columns. Mixed types allowed: numeric (quantitative binary), logical, factor, character. n Integer, number rows simulated output. Defaults `nrow(data)`. col_types Optional named list overriding auto-detected column types. entry must one `"quantitative"`, `"integer"`, `"binary"`, `"categorical"`. Whole-number columns auto-detected `"integer"`; override `"quantitative"` keep fractional (float) representation. marginal_resolution Integer, number quantile knots used represent quantitative column's marginal CDF. Default 128. Storing quantile grid (rather raw vector) privacy guarantee continuous columns. missingness One `"preserve_pattern"` (default), `"MCAR"`, `"none"`. `"preserve_pattern"`, NA-indicator matrix modeled second Gaussian copula co-missing variables stay co-missing. inject Optional named list phenotype specifications (phenotype injection). See **Details** **Examples**. copula_regularization Currently unused; reserved alternate PD-projection strategies. default `Matrix::nearPD()` via `"nearPD"`. privacy list privacy knobs: * `min_cell_size` (default 10): categorical levels fewer   many observations collapsed `""`. 5 requires   `force = TRUE` emits warning. * `drop_near_constant` (default `TRUE`): drop columns fewer 2   distinct non-NA values. * `max_missingness` (default 0.90): drop columns missingness rate   threshold (faithfully simulated   extreme tail re-identification risk). * `noise_on_corr` (default 0): standard deviation Gaussian noise   added -diagonals latent correlation matrix. Positive   values give differential-privacy-flavored guarantee; 0 means none. * `force` (default `FALSE`): set `TRUE` override   `min_cell_size < 5` safety check. seed Optional integer. non-`NULL`, seeds (PIT jitter, phenotype injection, MVN draw, missingness mask) stages independently output reproducible. global RNG touched `NULL`. verbose `TRUE` (default), prints progress privacy-audit line summarising dropped collapsed. stratify_by Optional character length 1 naming column stratify . set, simulator fits separate copula within level column concatenates per-stratum sims, preserving within-group joint distributions single copula average away. Useful categorical column drives big mean shifts continuous columns (e.g., `Species` `iris`). combined `inject` version. min_strata_n Integer minimum number rows required per stratum fitting. Strata skipped warning. Default 30. max_ordinal_levels Integer, default 10. `"integer"` column many distinct values (every value occurring least `min_cell_size` times) modeled ordinal trait exact level frequencies; otherwise uses quantile grid rounded nearest integer output. Either way output integer-typed.

title: Simulate a synthetic dataset preserving marginals and joint correlations — simulate_dataset
text: object class `"synthetica_sim"` (list) elements: * `data` – synthetic `data.frame` (n x p) * `marginals` – fitted marginals, one per column * `corr` – projected latent correlation matrix * `missingness` – missingness fit (rates , applicable, latent copula) * `inject_truth` – per-phenotype bookkeeping (`NULL` phenotype injected) * `dropped_cols`, `dropped_near_constant`, `dropped_high_missing` –    audit trail guardrail actions * `audit` – one-line summary string guardrail actions * `call` – matched call

title: Simulate a synthetic dataset preserving marginals and joint correlations — simulate_dataset
text: **Method.** simulator fits per-column marginal (empirical quantile grid quantitative columns; level proportions binary categorical) maps observation standard normal via probability integral transform (continuity-corrected discrete variables). Pairwise-complete Pearson correlation latent scores projected nearest positive-definite correlation matrix (`Matrix::nearPD()`). Synthetic rows drawn `MVN(0, R)` back-transformed column-wise stored marginals. Missingness modeled second Gaussian copula NA-indicator matrix. **Phenotype injection.** entry `inject` list fields: * `type` – `"quantitative"`, `"binary"`, `"categorical"` * `mean`, `sd` (quantitative); `prob` (binary); **** `levels` (character,   length >= 2) `probs` (proportions, length, > 0, summing 1)   `"categorical"` * `effect = list(kind, ..., )` `kind` `"mean_shift"` (  `size_sd` gives standardized effect) `"r_squared"` (  `min_r2` gives target R^2 latent scale), `` one   `""`, numeric fraction `(0, 1]` selecting random subset   target features, character vector feature names. Categorical   phenotypes support `kind = "r_squared"` version. Phenotypes generated rank-standardized latent scale back-transformed user-facing marginal. feasibility requested effect enforced (`rho' R_X^-1 rho <= 1`); violation, targets scaled warning emitted. `"categorical"` phenotype **ordinal** trait: single latent correlated targets cut cumulative-probability thresholds `probs` ordered `levels` (first level low end gradient). Binary two-level special case. thresholding attenuates latent correlation, realized per-target Spearman association reported empirically `inject_truth$<name>$realized_cor`. **Binary fidelity.** Injected binary (categorical/continuous) phenotypes generating latent spliced copula, designed latent correlation captured exactly; single, unavoidable thresholding factor `phi(c) / sqrt(p(1-p))` remains binaries, reported `inject_truth$<name>$binary_sim_factor`. Input ordered-discrete columns latent correlations recovered associations preserved rather attenuated round-trip: binary-like columns (numeric 0/1 column two-level factor M/F) via tetrachoric / biserial, ordered multi-level columns (ordinal-mode integers, ordered factors) via polychoric / polyserial. recoveries assume thresholded bivariate-normal latent. **Limitations.** Categorical phenotype injection ordinal; nominal grouping factor whose levels perturb different features independently, use [add_group_effect()] (post-processor). Unordered (>2-level) categorical *input* columns polychoric-corrected (inherent order), categorical input features supported effect targets version.

title: Simulate a synthetic dataset preserving marginals and joint correlations — simulate_dataset
code: set.seed(1) input <- data.frame(   x   = rnorm(200, 50, 10),   y   = rlnorm(200, log(20), 0.5),   sex = sample(c(0L, 1L), 200, replace = TRUE),   grp = factor(sample(letters[1:3], 200, replace = TRUE)) ) input$x[sample(200, 30)] <- NA  # Baseline simulation: synthetic copy sim <- simulate_dataset(input, n = 500, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE) str(sim$data) #> 'data.frame':	500 obs. of  4 variables: #>  $ x  : num  47.9 45.5 43.2 42.5 NA ... #>  $ y  : num  22.8 13.4 18.5 18.9 14.4 ... #>  $ sex: int  1 1 1 0 1 1 0 1 0 1 ... #>  $ grp: Factor w/ 3 levels "a","b","c": 2 1 2 3 1 2 3 2 3 2 ...  # Phenotype injection: a synthetic age column correlated with all features sim2 <- simulate_dataset(   input,   n = 500, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE,   inject = list(     age = list(       type = "quantitative", mean = 55, sd = 12,       effect = list(kind = "mean_shift", size_sd = 0.2, on = "all")     )   ) ) #> Warning: phenotype 'age': dropped 1 categorical target(s) -- not supported as effect targets in v1 sim2$inject_truth$age$mean_abs_real_cor #> [1] 0.1681992

title: Simulate Longitudinal / Repeated-Measures Data — simulate_longitudinal
text: Generate privacy-preserving synthetic copies longitudinal (panel) data – repeated measures subjects time – identifying subject `id` column `time` column (e.g. `Chick` `Time` `datasets::ChickWeight`).

title: Simulate Longitudinal / Repeated-Measures Data — simulate_longitudinal
code: simulate_longitudinal(   data,   id,   time,   n = NULL,   static = NULL,   id_prefix = "subj",   verbose = TRUE,   ... )

title: Simulate Longitudinal / Repeated-Measures Data — simulate_longitudinal
text: data long-format `data.frame` one row per (subject, time). id Character, name subject identifier column. time Character, name time column. unique observed values define time grid; genuinely irregular/continuous times binned common grid calling. n Number synthetic **subjects** generate. Default `NULL` uses number input subjects. static Optional character vector time-invariant covariate columns (constant within subject, e.g. `Diet`). `NULL` (default) auto-detects . Columns declared static actually vary within subject emit warning use first value per subject. id_prefix Character prefix synthetic subject ids. Default `"subj"` (ids become `subj1`, `subj2`, ...). verbose Logical; passed [simulate_dataset()]. ... arguments forwarded [simulate_dataset()] (`seed`, `privacy`, `missingness`, `marginal_resolution`, `max_ordinal_levels`, ...).

title: Simulate Longitudinal / Repeated-Measures Data — simulate_longitudinal
text: object class `"synthetica_long"`: list * `data` – synthetic long-format `data.frame` (columns input) * `wide` – underlying `synthetica_sim` fitted wide frame * `id`, `time`, `times`, `static`, `features`, `n_subjects` – metadata

title: Simulate Longitudinal / Repeated-Measures Data — simulate_longitudinal
text: data reshaped **wide** format (one row per subject, column time-varying feature timepoint), passed [simulate_dataset()], reshaped back long. turns within-subject temporal autocorrelation ordinary -column correlation, full copula machinery applies free: per-timepoint marginals capture trajectory shape (e.g. growth), missingness model reproduces dropout, time-invariant covariates ride along single columns, integer / discrete / polychoric handling work. handles ChickWeight-style panels, /RCTs (two timepoints), crossover trials (columns per period), modest biomarker panels. **** scale high-dimensional longitudinal 'omics (warning fires `features x timepoints` exceeds hundred columns), needs different method.

title: Simulate Longitudinal / Repeated-Measures Data — simulate_longitudinal
code: data(ChickWeight, package = "datasets") sim <- simulate_longitudinal(ChickWeight, id = "Chick", time = "Time",                              seed = 1, verbose = FALSE) head(sim$data) #>   weight Time Chick Diet #> 1     43    0 subj1    1 #> 2     51    2 subj1    1 #> 3     61    4 subj1    1 #> 4     72    6 subj1    1 #> 5     84    8 subj1    1 #> 6     87   10 subj1    1 # synthetic chicks have growing weight trajectories and a Diet covariate
